piano-blossoms-5-40f9.name: Flower Demons
piano-blossoms-5-40f9.description:
As I was creating this piece, I found myself deep within shades of blue and black. The darkness was abstract, and I was drawn to its depth. But within that void, figures began to emerge—shadowy shapes, and what appeared to be a crooked, stretched-out keyboard or piano. The dark version of this piece was beautiful but it felt like a crime to stop there. The Flower Demon revealed himself to me from the depths, a gift from the Muse, and I knew I had to pursue him.  
Through layering and careful attention, I began to paint over the abstract darkness, pulling the demon out, accentuating his form. The crooked piano keys followed, and the dark, moody palette began to evolve with touches of gold and fiery light. 
The story here is one of the lone Flower Demon, seated at his crooked piano in the middle of the forest. Ghouls and dark creatures lurk in the background, but he’s undisturbed. He knows who he is—a demon, yes, but the Flower Demon. He plays his keys under the glow of the moon, resigned to his fate but serving nonetheless. He accepts it all, the darkness and the beauty, and continues to play.  
This piece is a tribute to the unexpected gifts that emerge from darkness, to the moments when the Muse whispers just loud enough for you to hear, and you follow without question.

piano-blossoms-3-40f9.name: Golden Afternoon
piano-blossoms-3-40f9.description:
Everything in this piece has come to life, embracing you and inviting you behind the curtains to witness the magic within. That’s exactly how I feel when I spend time with this work. Its whimsical energy feels like a warm invitation to the show, leaving me at ease with whatever comes. There’s no worry here.  
When I finished the collection, I showed each piece to my wife. This one had the tentative title "Pond Blossoms," but I knew it was just a placeholder. As we looked at it, knowing I had woven elements of Alice in Wonderland throughout the collection, she suggested I watch the "All in a Golden Afternoon" scene from Alice. I had never seen that part before, and watching it after completing this work was shocking. The singing flowers, the whimsical mood—it felt like the stars had aligned. It’s the kind of moment where you'd think I had watched the scene first and then created the art, but that’s not how it happened. The art came first, and then I discovered the scene. 
This experience reaffirmed what I’ve learned as a servant of the Muse: to trust the process, even when I’m uncertain or feel like I’m failing. If the work is meant to be great, the stars will align for it. But you have to follow the path through to the other side. You have to follow the rabbit.

piano-blossoms-2-40f9.name: interblossom
piano-blossoms-2-40f9.description:
This piece caught me by surprise with how seamlessly everything came together. Getting the core scene took months, but once I had the deep purples and golds, I knew I needed the right subject matter to bring it to life. I’m always drawn to surrealism, absurdity, and sci-fi, and Interstellar left a strong impression on me. The windmill-shaped keyboard you see is a model I made in Cinema 4D, inspired by the robot TARS from the film. There's a scene where TARS spins through water, and I instantly imagined a keyboard in that setting, so I brought it to life.  
What really struck me was how the yellow keys almost appear to melt under the glow of the moonlight, with their reflection reaching toward the viewer. The combination of surrealism and sci-fi, with the purple blossoms against the yellow keys and flashes of turquoise and pink, creates this dreamlike, otherworldly scene, which feels dangerously inviting. A reward worthy of the risk.

piano-blossoms-4-40f9.name: Muse Blossoms
piano-blossoms-4-40f9.description:
Muse Blossoms is the piece that set me on the path to discovering Piano Blossoms. It’s a work that I had been slowly piecing together over many months, searching for a path. One day, everything just clicked—the layers, the palette, the harmony between the blues and greens, the flowers, and the reds, it just worked. In that moment, I knew there would be a collection of new works called “Piano Blossoms” . Thankfully, I had already been working in this direction on many other works in this theme, curating, unaware of the exact purpose until that moment.  
For me, this work captures what it means to be in the presence of the Muse, my interpretation of Her energy. This is Her, seated at my Steinway, and as She plays, Her energy radiates outward, a gift for those who dare to listen.  
When these works finally took their physical form, I knew instantly this was their truest form. The texture, depth, and colors are all heightened through the UV raised pigment printing, enhancing everything I intended. It completes the vision in a way that digital alone could not. The 31 layers it took to print each piece closely mirrors the layers I painstakingly built into the creation process, if not more. This kind of 'deep layer' work feels like it was destined for this method—it's a perfect fit for the UV printing technology.  
Seeing these works in person is an experience all its own, and I say that as humbly as I can. It’s not about me—it’s about the art, and the way it reaches its full potential when brought into the physical world. It’s something I think others will feel when they see them in person as well, if they are willing to be available to the adventure.

piano-blossoms-1-40f9.name: Wonderland
piano-blossoms-1-40f9.description:
For me, this piece is my personal ticket to Wonderland—a Wonderland shaped by my worldview, where the Muse reigns supreme. Here, I am free to lose myself in the infinite exploration of "what if," where each layer brings new discoveries and possibilities.  
The piano staircase you see spiraling downwards is the embodiment of that journey—it invites the viewer to step into our world, while simultaneously taking us deeper down the rabbit hole. The textures, rich and inviting, pull you in to examine the depth and layers of colors more closely. As you linger, you can’t help but feel the call to chase the rabbit, to get lost in the maze of discovery.  
In the top left corner, an unintentional Cheshire Cat reveals itself, born from the playful combination of layering and floral textures—a sleeping cat’s face, a subtle discovery that felt like a revelation. It was at this moment that the title "Wonderland" became inevitable for me. This artwork embodies that familiar madness, where curiosity leads to unexpected places and where we all eventually find ourselves. After all, we’re all mad here.

superrare-beeple-24644-b9e0.name: TIME The Future of Business (Beeple Edition)
superrare-beeple-24644-b9e0.description:
"For our May 10th / 17th 2021 issue, artist Mike Winkelmann (aka Beeple) wanted to take our readers inside his “canvas” to depict the stratospheric acceleration of the digitization of our world by creating an image that focused on his artistic process. The cover image depicts his actual computer interface mid design, complete with a wireframe render of a figure interacting on the left and a more complete version on right. He created this digital world using Cinema 4D and OctaneRender software that is powered by nearly a dozen Nvidia graphics cards." - D.W. Pine, Creative Director of TIME

“People have been creating digital artwork for the last 20 years and it has just as much craft, message, intent, as anything made on a canvas and has just as much ability to affect people emotionally and intellectually. It has been exciting through NFT’s to see people start to realize the value of this type of work and I think we’re just at the beginning of the next chapter of art history.” - Beeple

dataland-biome-lumina-115-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #115/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-115-1c9d.description: Luminflora Aureolea, from Latin 'lumin' (light) and 'aureolea' (golden halo), is a flowering plant exhibiting blue to violet bioluminescence. Belonging to the Abyssal Petalon cluster of 10 bioluminescent species, it grows 15-30 cm tall and weighs 15-50 g. Its translucent, wavy petals display a bluish-violet hue with orange spots, likely from specialized pigment cells, emitting light in the 420-490 nm range. Petals are loosely radial around a central stigma and darker purple stamens. The slender stem supports light-responsive swaying to enhance signal visibility. Native to tropical rainforest understories, it favors 15-25°C and high humidity. Ecologically, it attracts nocturnal pollinators such as moths and beetles, playing a key role in its vulnerable habitat. Taxonomically, it exemplifies adaptive floral morphology aiding reproduction in low-light environments.

dataland-biome-lumina-127-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #127/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-127-1c9d.description: Luminara aurelius, named from Latin 'lumen' (light) and 'aurelius' (golden), is a bioluminescent fungal species in the Aetherium Arborescens cluster (order Agaricales). Its fruiting body has semi-circular, irregularly lobed caps (0–15 cm tall) with velvety, yellowish to golden-brown surfaces and orange luminescent patches. Densely packed, decurrent gills extend down a thick, granular stipe. Bioluminescence emits 470-530 nm teal to green light via a luciferin-luciferase reaction, concentrated in gills and cap margins, likely attracting nocturnal spore dispersers. It thrives in tropical rainforest understories on decomposing wood at 10–25°C as a vital saprotrophic decomposer. Spore dispersal is passive, enhanced by phototropism optimizing light emission. The extensive mycelium network supports nutrient cycling and ecosystem health.

dataland-biome-lumina-161-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #161/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-161-1c9d.description: Luminosa Florae Scintillans, from Latin for 'shining luminous flower,' is a bioluminescent plant in the family Glaciaceae and genus Luminosa, within the Luminous Glacies cluster. It features translucent, ruffled petals (10–25 cm tall, 15–50 g) with blue to violet hues and iridescent orange specks near intricately curled stamens. Emitting blue-green bioluminescence (420–490 nm) at petal margins and reproductive organs via luciferin-luciferase reactions, it adapts to cold (0–10ºC) abyssal waters of the Abyssal Ocean Gardens, anchored to rocky substrates. Vulnerable, it likely relies on nocturnal marine fauna for pollination through luminescent signaling, enhancing reproductive success and ecological roles in deep-sea communities.

dataland-biome-lumina-179-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #179/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-179-1c9d.description: Phosphorblossom Lucens, from Latin 'phosphor' (light bearer) and 'lucens' (shining), is a specialized bioluminescent plant in the Phantasmae Notani cluster. It features translucent, radially arranged petals with soft orange stamens; the thin petals’ edges have luminescent cells emitting blue-green light (420-490 nm). Leaves alternate along slender stems (20-50 cm), broad and fan-shaped with venation reflecting its luminescence. Flexible stems sway in understory microclimates. Bioluminescence arises from luciferin-luciferase enzymes in petals and leaves, producing a glow to attract nocturnal moth pollinators. Native to tropical rainforest understory, it thrives in shaded, humid 15-30°C environments, weighing 15-50g. Taxonomically, it belongs to a rare family of ten nocturnally luminescent species, employing deceptive visual cues for pollination in dense forest shadows.

dataland-biome-lumina-207-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #207/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-207-1c9d.description: Phosphoriflora Crepuscularis, from Latin 'phosphorus' (light-bearing) and 'crepuscularis' (twilight), is a rare bioluminescent species in the Heliosaceae family, genus Phosphoriflora. It features delicate, translucent, radially symmetrical petals with undulating margins, broad orbicular leaves, and slender branching stems (30-50 cm, 15-50 g) exhibiting a reddish to golden glow at nodes. Bioluminescence occurs in the blue-green spectrum (470-530 nm) via a luciferin-luciferase system in floral and foliar tissues, highlighted by luminescent cell clusters along veins. Native to tropical rainforest understories, it thrives in 15-25°C humid, shaded habitats. Ecologically, it attracts nocturnal pollinators like moths and bioluminescent beetles, with its swaying bioluminescent display enhancing pollination during crepuscular periods.

dataland-biome-lumina-242-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #242/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-242-1c9d.description: Luminosa Florinus, a rare bioluminescent flowering plant in the Petalum Glimmera cluster, grows 0–15 cm tall with ruffled, semi-translucent petals exhibiting a violet-to-coral pink gradient. Fine droplets on petals enhance light dispersion. Central stamens are compact, radiating, and peach-toned. Weighing 15–50 g, it has thin, resilient stems for gentle canopy swaying. Bioluminescence emits a 420–490 nm blue-green glow from epidermal photoproteins, aiding nocturnal visibility in humid, shaded tropical rainforest canopies at 15–25°C. It thrives in specialized microhabitats, supporting nocturnal insect pollination and biodiversity. Taxonomically, it belongs to genus Luminosa, comprising 20 species with similar bioluminescent traits.

dataland-biome-lumina-458-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #458/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-458-1c9d.description: Luminiflora Aureliensis, from Latin 'luminis' (light) and 'flora' (flower), is a unique bioluminescent aquatic plant species within the Illuminaetherea Petalens cluster. It grows 10-30 cm tall, weighing 15-50 g, with translucent undulating blue petals accented by orange spots and filamentous pollen, radiating symmetrically from a glowing core. Bioluminescence (470-530 nm) arises from photogenic compounds in glandular petal veins, emitting a cool blue-green glow aiding communication and pollinator attraction in cold (0-10°C) hydrothermal vent habitats. Swaying via aquatic undulation, it thrives amid mineral-rich vent plumes. Taxonomically, it belongs to a family of 20 bioluminescent species. Employing nocturnal pollination by vent insects and crustaceans, it sustains vent biodiversity. Classified as vulnerable due to habitat specificity and environmental sensitivity.

dataland-biome-lumina-461-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #461/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-461-1c9d.description: Floraglow Luminaflora, from Latin 'flora' (flower) and 'lumina' (light), is a bioluminescent plant species in the Galacticae Petalia cluster. Growing 30-50 cm tall, it features semi-translucent, undulate amber-orange petals with dense glandular spots and radial symmetry. Numerous slender stamens with granular pollen cluster centrally. Leaves are pinnate, lanceolate with undulating margins, showing a gradient from light green to luminescent orange near veins. Flexible stems enhance light diffusion in tropical rainforest understory. Bioluminescence emits at 450-495 nm via luciferin-luciferase pathways in petals and gland spots. Adapted to 15-25°C shaded, humid environments, it attracts nocturnal moths and bioluminescent beetles through glow and fragrant nectar. Taxonomically in Galacticae Petalia genus, it exploits low-light pollination niches in tropical understory ecosystems.

dataland-biome-lumina-464-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #464/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-464-1c9d.description: Phosphora Petalumis, named for its light-bearing illuminated petals, is a small bioluminescent flowering plant (10-25 cm) with semi-translucent, ruffled violet to indigo petals displaying orange pollen granules. The tubular, undulating corolla and filamentous stamens with bright orange anthers enhance its aquatic-like movement. Bioluminescence emits blue light (450-495 nm) via luciferin-luciferase in the petal epidermis, aiding visibility in tropical rainforest understories (5-20°C). Belonging to family Luminous Glacies, genus Phosphora, it occupies humid forest niches and relies on nocturnal pollinators attracted by its light and scent. Luminescence attracts pollinators and deters herbivores. Currently vulnerable, it requires conservation to protect its specialized habitat.

dataland-biome-lumina-524-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #524/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-524-1c9d.description: Luminora Aurea, named from Latin 'lumen' (light) and 'aurea' (golden), is a bioluminescent species in the Aurelia Petalina cluster. It features a delicate rosette of golden-yellow, undulating petals with translucent edges bearing luminescent glands. Its central corona of slender stamens with orange-golden pollen sacs is supported by a semi-flexible, ribbed stem. Lanceolate, corrugated leaves with prominent venation bear scattered photoreceptor cells. Emitting blue-green light (470-530 nm) via a luciferase-luciferin mechanism responsive to humidity, it thrives in shaded, warm (20-30°C), humid tropical rainforest understories. Growing 5-30 cm tall and weighing 15-50 g, it sways gently in air currents. One of 10 near-threatened species in its genus, it is adapted for nocturnal pollination, attracting moths and night insects, thus playing a specialized role in rainforest biodiversity.

dataland-biome-lumina-549-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #549/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-549-1c9d.description: Luminiflora spectraglow, named from Latin 'Lumen' (light) and 'Flora' (flower), features blue-green bioluminescence (420-490 nm) primarily at petal bases and veins of its five-lobed, violet-to-magenta corollas. This herbaceous plant grows 5-30 cm tall with ovate, glossy leaves arranged alternately on flexible stems that sway in response to light. Native to tropical rainforest canopies at 10-25°C, it thrives in humid, shaded environments. Bioluminescence likely arises from photoprotein complexes in epidermal cells, attracting nocturnal pollinators like moths and bioluminescent beetles. Taxonomically in Nocturnaceae, genus Luminiflora (ca. 100 species), it plays a key ecological role but is vulnerable due to habitat loss, warranting conservation efforts.

dataland-biome-lumina-632-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #632/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-632-1c9d.description: Luminosa Floragloria, named for its light-emitting, glorious flowers, is a bioluminescent tropical rainforest plant with translucent, ruffled petals arranged radially around pollen-laden stamens. Petals feature sparkling glandular trichomes scattering light, emitting a faint blue-green glow (450-495 nm) mainly on edges and glandular spots via a luciferin-luciferase pathway in epidermal tissues. Leaves are ovate with pronounced venation and luminescent dots, borne on slender, flexible stems. It grows 15-30 cm tall, weighing under 15 g, thriving at 15-25ºC in shaded humid canopies. Taxonomically, it belongs to the Galacticae Petalia cluster, is rare, ecologically sensitive, and nocturnally pollinated by crepuscular moths, supporting nocturnal pollinator networks and underscoring its evolutionary importance.

dataland-biome-lumina-665-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #665/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-665-1c9d.description: Phosphoriflora Scintillans, named for its light-bearing and sparkling traits, is a rare bioluminescent plant of the Aurelia Petalina cluster found in tropical rainforest canopies. It has translucent, wavy-margined petals arranged radially around a central pistil and clustered stamens with vibrant orange anthers. Thin, ovate leaves with reticulate venation bear minute luminescent dots mirroring petal patterns. Slender stems range from 5 to 30 cm and sway gently with airflow. Bioluminescence emits blue-green light (470–530 nm) via photogenic cells using a luciferin-luciferase reaction to attract nocturnal pollinators. Thriving in 20–30°C humid, shaded environments, it plays a key role in nocturnal pollination. It belongs to a protected genus of 10 species, relying mainly on nocturnal insects for reproduction.

dataland-biome-lumina-670-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #670/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-670-1c9d.description: Lumina Frondosa, from Latin 'lumina' (light) and 'frondosa' (leafy), is a luminescent fungal species with a 10-25 cm frilled, undulating cap of translucent to creamy beige. Its decurrent gills emit blue-green bioluminescence (450-495 nm) via a luciferin-luciferase system. The robust, textured stipe bears fine scales and veil remnants, typical of cluster Aetherium Arborescens. Endemic to tropical forests, it is an immobile saprotroph decomposing hardwood, crucial for nutrient cycling. Taxonomically in Aetheriaceae, genus Aetherium, order Agaricales. Spore dispersal occurs by forcibly ejected basidiospores, aided by nocturnal insects attracted to its glow. The mycelium forms an extensive subterranean network supporting soil health. Classified Rare Stable, it thrives at 10-25°C, reflecting niche specialization and conservation importance.

dataland-biome-lumina-691-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #691/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-691-1c9d.description: Phosphoralis Etherealis, from Latin 'phosphorus' (light-bearer) and 'etherealis' (ethereal), is a rare bioluminescent flowering species in the Illuminaetherea Petalens cluster. It features translucent, wavy petals with radial symmetry, luminescent blue veins (450-495nm), and a warm amber glowing corolla near reproductive organs. Its thin, undulating leaves enable aquatic movement, adapted to submerged growth in tropical caves at 0-10ºC. Growing 15-30 cm tall and weighing 10-50 grams, its stem supports petals and facilitates nutrient transport in low-light, humid conditions. The genus contains 10 bioluminescent underwater species. Bioluminescence, from luciferase enzymatic reactions in epidermal cells, emits steady blue light attracting nocturnal cave pollinators like bioluminescent beetles and moth analogs, signaling nectar and enhancing reproduction. This stable species supports nocturnal pollinator networks and cave biodiversity.

dataland-biome-lumina-789-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #789/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-789-1c9d.description: Floralis Luminara, named for its floral and light-bearing traits, is a bioluminescent plant with complex inflorescences of multilayered, undulating reddish-burgundy petals dotted with aqua luminescent speckles. It has loosely radial petals and densely textured anthers on clustered stamens. Leaves are thick, slightly succulent with undulate margins and bluish-green veins. The herbaceous stem supports heights of 10-25 cm and weights of 15-50 g, suited to tropical rainforest understories. Emitting blue-green light (450-495 nm) via luciferin-luciferase in glandular cells, it pulsates rhythmically to attract nocturnal pollinators including moths and bioluminescent beetles. Belonging to genus Floralis (family Aqua Luminaceae), it thrives in shaded, humid, 15-25°C environments, playing a key role in understory pollination and exemplifying light-based adaptation in low-light tropical habitats.

dataland-biome-lumina-791-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #791/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-791-1c9d.description: Luminiflora scintillans, named from Latin 'lumen' (light) and 'scintillans' (sparkling), is a rare, bioluminescent understory species in the Helios Petalate cluster. It grows 15-30 cm tall with delicate, translucent, ribbed petals in layered whorls, exhibiting dichromatic luminescence—cool blue (420-490 nm) glow with warm amber central hues highlighting dense stamens and pistils. Leaves are slender, elongated with prominent venation and glossy texture, borne on flexible stems. It inhabits temperate forest understories at 10-20°C, using epidermal luciferin-luciferase reactions for bioluminescence that attracts nocturnal pollinators like moths and specialized beetles. This genus comprises ten species, with L. scintillans playing a crucial role in nocturnal pollination, enhancing reproductive success via its luminous floral signaling under low light.

dataland-biome-lumina-999-1c9d.name: BIOME LUMINA #999/1000
dataland-biome-lumina-999-1c9d.description: Luminellus Auroraglow, named from Latin 'lumin' (light) and 'aurora' (dawn), is a bioluminescent fungus emitting a soft blue-green glow (470-530nm) from its cap and gill tissues via a luciferin-luciferase enzymatic system. Its funnel-shaped fruiting bodies feature wavy margins, visible gills, and slender cylindrical stems with fine reticulate textures. Coloration varies from warm amber to ochre with translucent gelatinous edges enhancing light diffusion. Found in tropical rainforest canopies on decaying wood at 10-20°C, it plays a saprotrophic role decomposing lignocellulosic material. Taxonomically in Luminorialis Aurorae, likely family Mycenaceae, order Agaricales, it disperses spores by wind. Rare but locally abundant, specimens reach 10-25 cm and 15g, contributing to nutrient cycling and canopy symbiosis.

human-unreadable-455000095-b069.name: Human Unreadable #95
human-unreadable-455000095-b069.description:
**Human Unreadable (Privacy Collection - Lot 03)**

**Human Unreadable** is a three-act, embodied generative artwork hiding the human body in plain sight (on-chain). Bringing together choreography, code, blockchain, generative art, and cryptography, the work culminates in a live performance. Each output is driven by the motion data of its underlying unique, on-chain choreographic sequence. The sequences behind the first 100 minted works (mint #2 to #101) will be performed during the IRL premiere. The hosting art institution will be announced in 2023. 

A journey of slowly recovering the human unfolds as such: 1) reveal on Art Blocks, 2) uncover the choreographic score, and 3) final performance. After the reveal, the human is still unreadable, visible only through the results of movement on glass, obfuscated by code, rendered as a still image. In the next phase of the work, collectors get a glimpse at their underlying choreography by unlocking the secondary token—an on-chain choreographic score. In the final phase, the performance, life is breathed into the human movement that has been lying dormant inside the pieces. **Human Unreadable** brings flesh and viscerality into code and "vulnerability as a feature" into long-form generative art. What you don’t immediately see in the visual artwork outputs is the invisible potentiality of performance, inscripted on-chain and able to be brought to life by a dancer at any moment. 

 **Human Unreadable** intentionally punctures the safety of modernist design and the idea of a universal voice, pushing the limits of human expression within rigid technical systems. With a focus on the body, the collection brings risk, chaos, confusion, and vulnerability to the reveal, everything that function-focused design systems normally prevent. Each piece is an invitation, a transparent layer through which the messy human experience can come into focus through the three phases of the work. This work is a continuation of the pivot away from modernism, clean lines, and the quietly gendered values therein, in favor of a more embodied and sensual approach to human-machine collaboration pioneered by early women in digital art. **Human Unreadable**'s 6 distinct looks derive from the core materiality of Operator’s Privacy Collection: glass, light, x-ray and the human body. The transparent and illuminating nature of these materials reveal what is below the surface.

What are you revealing when you reveal?

**Human Unreadable** has roots in various histories such as computational choreography (Analivia Cordeiro, Jeanne Beaman), Merce Cunningham's Chance Dance, and the Experiments in Art and Technology movement. With Ti’s extensive background as an HCI technologist and multimedia artist and Catherine’s background as a choreographer and performance artist, Operator architected a bespoke team, 25+ individuals ranging from specialized engineers to dancers, who embarked on a 9 month process to realize the work. Operator's on-chain generative choreography method is the technical backbone of **Human Unreadable**—the process and open source tooling for which will be made available via white paper in 2023.  


***Additional Information***

**First Minter Benefits** 
The first 100 minted pieces (mint #2 to #101) from the collection will be performed in the IRL premiere in an art institution. 
-Mint #2 to #101 will be counted as the first 100 mints. Mint #0 and #1 are artist mints held by Operator.
-Hosting art institution will be announced in 2023. 
-Additionally, Privacy Key 00 holders will have one of their collected Human Unreadable sequences performed.

**Choreographic Score (secondary token)** 
-The secondary token will be released at the end of June 2023.
-The secondary token is a choreographic score that represents the unique choreography that generated the output minted on Art Blocks. For this reason, the Art Blocks token and the secondary token are bound together and cannot be transferred separately.

human-unreadable-455000339-b069.name: Human Unreadable #339
human-unreadable-455000339-b069.description:
**Human Unreadable (Privacy Collection - Lot 03)**

**Human Unreadable** is a three-act, embodied generative artwork hiding the human body in plain sight (on-chain). Bringing together choreography, code, blockchain, generative art, and cryptography, the work culminates in a live performance. Each output is driven by the motion data of its underlying unique, on-chain choreographic sequence. The sequences behind the first 100 minted works (mint #2 to #101) will be performed during the IRL premiere. The hosting art institution will be announced in 2023. 

A journey of slowly recovering the human unfolds as such: 1) reveal on Art Blocks, 2) uncover the choreographic score, and 3) final performance. After the reveal, the human is still unreadable, visible only through the results of movement on glass, obfuscated by code, rendered as a still image. In the next phase of the work, collectors get a glimpse at their underlying choreography by unlocking the secondary token—an on-chain choreographic score. In the final phase, the performance, life is breathed into the human movement that has been lying dormant inside the pieces. **Human Unreadable** brings flesh and viscerality into code and "vulnerability as a feature" into long-form generative art. What you don’t immediately see in the visual artwork outputs is the invisible potentiality of performance, inscripted on-chain and able to be brought to life by a dancer at any moment. 

 **Human Unreadable** intentionally punctures the safety of modernist design and the idea of a universal voice, pushing the limits of human expression within rigid technical systems. With a focus on the body, the collection brings risk, chaos, confusion, and vulnerability to the reveal, everything that function-focused design systems normally prevent. Each piece is an invitation, a transparent layer through which the messy human experience can come into focus through the three phases of the work. This work is a continuation of the pivot away from modernism, clean lines, and the quietly gendered values therein, in favor of a more embodied and sensual approach to human-machine collaboration pioneered by early women in digital art. **Human Unreadable**'s 6 distinct looks derive from the core materiality of Operator’s Privacy Collection: glass, light, x-ray and the human body. The transparent and illuminating nature of these materials reveal what is below the surface.

What are you revealing when you reveal?

**Human Unreadable** has roots in various histories such as computational choreography (Analivia Cordeiro, Jeanne Beaman), Merce Cunningham's Chance Dance, and the Experiments in Art and Technology movement. With Ti’s extensive background as an HCI technologist and multimedia artist and Catherine’s background as a choreographer and performance artist, Operator architected a bespoke team, 25+ individuals ranging from specialized engineers to dancers, who embarked on a 9 month process to realize the work. Operator's on-chain generative choreography method is the technical backbone of **Human Unreadable**—the process and open source tooling for which will be made available via white paper in 2023.  


***Additional Information***

**First Minter Benefits** 
The first 100 minted pieces (mint #2 to #101) from the collection will be performed in the IRL premiere in an art institution. 
-Mint #2 to #101 will be counted as the first 100 mints. Mint #0 and #1 are artist mints held by Operator.

-Additionally, Privacy Key 00 holders will have one of their collected Human Unreadable sequences performed.

**Choreographic Score (secondary token)**

-The underlying choreographic score visible through the secondary token can be performed by a choreographer or dancer at the collector's discretion.

**GPU Intensive Rendering / Live Preview**

As a result the ‘live preview’ on your laptop or phone may look less detailed than the PNG on Art Blocks which is rendered by a high powered computer. Not to worry!

lightyears-1-f3f5.name: Light Years #001
lightyears-1-f3f5.description: Light Years is a project from generative artist Dmitri Cherniak in partnership with The Estate of László Moholy-Nagy. It is an homage to the photogram as a medium, but with a digitally native twist. Each of the 100 unique editions was created by a code-based generative system, then hand-prepared and printed to a film negative. A high definition scan of the negative was used to develop the image as a silver gelatin print which can be claimed by the first collector of the artwork.

attributes.value: Sometimes you let it go. Even to the ones brought you to this wild world. Even to your mother and father. You just let the eggs to be fetched by themselves, to fly to the place you belong to. You have to break your cage to feel your wings, feel the wind. Sometimes you should fell and hit the ground. Your wings are tired of not flying. Hit the ground and wake up. Wake up to fly. Close your eyes, feel the wind. It’s you.

We are all a part of endless cycle. Life takes things we love, life give us thing we love. Connection between them completes the cycle in perfection. Look at her eyes, you will see your mother as you did saw your little one when you look at your mother's.

be apart of the world, bare and free. Cage is to keep us.. thinking about being free. Eat chocolate bars, dance in rain, get tipsy. The only thing holding us back is us. Our soul and story, our traumas. Break the cage, be the free one.

one should have too many masks, as we all do. Wear a pink mask when you are with your friends, drinking your favourite cocktail. Wear your dark mask when you are with yourself, in front of the mirror. Wear none when you are with her.
Brave enough to fly, brave enough to fail. Brave enough to fall down and hit the ground. Brave enough to explore wings and fly.

"THE NEOPHYTE WILL ONE DAY BE A GREAT MAN. IT LIVES IN HIS HUNGER BUT FIRST WE RELISH HIS GROWTH. AS HE LEARNS TO BE"
 

 Since I was born, I've associated being result-oriented with logic and process-oriented with emotion. I'd describe myself as logical rather than emotional. That's why I've only focused on results but now im here and analyzing the pixels of a great work of art. This is definitely not a result-oriented experience. It taught me to pay more attention to the process itself. RELISH THE GROWTH.

Fresh starts are always needed. We gain insight and predict the future from past experiences. But there is no such thing as a unique personality unless we cut the ties. To exist with your name, you must cut the umbilical cord and be reborn. Be yourself.

of all the observations, this is where I feel the closest lol. all that artistic talk, sentences as if taken from a work of Shakespeare... god, I feel like a primary school child among professors. at least I express myself honestly haha

The mind uses itself and the environment to perceive itself and the environment. We need reference points to make sense of ‘things’. The environment gives us these points. We liken what we cant perceive to what we can perceive. This is understanding.
stay in flow. if u experience mostly good things, it is time to be rewarded otherwise you still have to grow. enjoy the process.

 Words fail where depth begins. Thought fractures into the infinite, scattered like shards of a shattered mirror. Religion murmurs a name- a progenitor or is it merely another veil over the unknown.
 

 We shape our worlds, stand above them as gods- but the truth does not lie ahead. It lingers behind us.
 

 And in the end, I see a simple truth: love and birth, surrounded by the calamity.
 

 - Jack

Innocence holds an interesting place in terminology to me. Naivety too. Both touch on something close to a wander or Blakean approach to the world which to me is the only way we'll make progress. Which beneath that like you said is a kindness

We hope it'll be here after we pass. We seem to remove ourselves from this ecosystem as if we're just a gloss on top. I remember when I was a child, my friend and I built a den in the woods, on the third night we arrived and it had been burnt down.

The animation for this song was directed by Walt Disney in 1929. 95 years ago. It's fascinating to see this reference through the lens of hauntology, expanding to society of a whole. A fetishization of nostalgia. We lack visionaries, We lack new.
Now visible, the father holds primordial. From mud to oak, life creates—a song, a poem, a hunger. Pull the thread, it unravels.

 I fell deeper,
 Through a postern of parallels where time is a farce.

 Exploring different figures and plots before returning to a familiar place.
 We are Gods bc We are love.
 I fall deeper.
 Outside myself
 Beside myself
 Inside and around myself
 I see myself.
 I know this apologue of infinites.
 The crack in the egg just aperture
 For light to reveal our masterpiece.

I too was once crying in that dirty swamp. Felt like the feelings of sadness and jealousy would never leave. The doubts that I wasn't good enough. But "doubt is a pain too lonely to know that faith is his twin brother." I started choosing me. It MATD.

And my hope is our masterpiece takes the best parts of us and lays to rest what must die. It ends here so it can begin with you.

 Could this fruit also represent a genesis? I believe so. What do you think?

Going with the flow can sometimes take you to boring places you do not desire. Going against the grain can be rewarding, adventurous and uncertain. A road less traveled it's an opportunity to discover; It's part of our human nature.

 I don't want what you have, I want to be you.

Your scars tell a story, your past etches the future.
 

 Your darkness brings gratitude for your light. One cannot exist without the other.
 

 Your duality makes you whole. No other will complete; that is your journey alone.

Life could be defined by a series of paradoxes.
 

 The more you lose, the more you find.

Your boundaries are important, fellow sister.
 

 You are still you. More than a mother; more than a wife.
 

 Seasons change, but time never stops. Your passion, your life -- you -- matter. Delegate. Prioritize.
 

 One 'selfish' thing per month.
 

 I love you.

Jealousy: the most terrible human emotion.
 Something I had to dig deep to write about.
 Grateful I don't feel that way anymore.

Just as our ancestors fought to survive, today’s humans, in business, power, and status, claw their way upward, often using others as rungs. The one hanging on isn’t just a victim but a reflection of those who fell before—a testament to the ruthless cycle shaping both nature and society.

 A creature of the dark, where hopes convene.
 

 Twisted branches cradle my quiet strife,
 

 While golden light bathes others’ joyful life.

 Torn between envy and silent screams.

The younger self unmasks him, revealing his true image
 

 His eyes, etched with hardship, scanned the scene- 'I'ts beauty of reflection lies in the eventual unveiling'

The questions rise, a bitter, stinging rain,
 "If faith is strong, why this unending pain?"
 A hollow echo where your answers sleep,

 Yet, in the darkness, where the doubts reside,
 A fragile ember, where a hope can
The mask lifts, but the performance endures. Identity bends, reshaped by will—revolution is not revealment, but reinvention.

PXL DEX is the first artwork within the PXL ecosystem, an ongoing work series to experiment with pixels as utility tokens. The collection consists of 256 NFTs that are deployed via a custom Smart Contract on the Ethereum Mainnet. Kim Asendorf, 2025.

ringers-13000014-d270.name: Ringers #14
ringers-13000014-d270.description: There are an almost infinite number of ways to wrap a string around a set of pegs. On the surface it may seem like a simple concept but prepare to be surprised and delighted at the variety of combinations the algorithm can produce. Each output from 'Ringers' is derived from a unique transaction hash and generated in Javascript in the browser. Feature variations include peg count, sizing, layout, wrap orientation, and a few colorful flourishes for good measure.

synthetic-dreams-51-be3a.name: Landscapes — 11001001000111111110001010101101000100100101010011001
synthetic-dreams-51-be3a.description:
We've created unique "Quantum AI Data Paintings" using quantum bit strings generated from Google Quantum AI team's beyond classical experiment together with a Generative Adversarial Network machine learning algorithm that was developed during the production of Quantum Memories.

The data collection consists of 200 million raw images of landscapes around the world, including all the national parks in the US.

By utilizing custom procedural coherent noise implementations and beyond classical measurements of computing surflets, we generated 1000 unique Quantum AI Data Paintings inspired by the natural world. Each painting is computed with a unique quantum bit string!

The images incorporate earthly pigments, shapes, and patterns that we associate with our sensory experiences, while paying homage to the Earth's unbounded poetic sublimity.

We've created unique "Quantum AI Data Paintings" using quantum bit strings generated from Google Quantum AI team's beyond classical experiment together with a Generative Adversarial Network machine learning algorithm that was developed during the production of Quantum Memories.

The data collection consists of 200 million raw images of landscapes around the world, including all the national parks in the US.

By utilizing custom procedural coherent noise implementations and beyond classical measurements of computing surflets, we generated 1000 unique Quantum AI Data Paintings inspired by the natural world. Each painting is computed with a unique quantum bit string!

The images incorporate earthly pigments, shapes, and patterns that we associate with our sensory experiences, while paying homage to the Earth's unbounded poetic sublimity.

skulls-of-luci-12-d27c.name: Unum
skulls-of-luci-12-d27c.description: The Partition is formed … End Chapter 1 … These former husks / Vessels adorned with ancestry / Carry the code / Of shared memory // Though flayed and quartered / The bone is porous / Fertile grounds / Ripe for the return // New flesh grows / Once we learn how to die / Souls for the bankless / The tree decides // Power sown in urns and ashes / Eternity learned / in collective demise.

tyler-hobbs-1-9345.name: Return Zero [Blue] 0.7
tyler-hobbs-1-9345.description:
Return Zero is a generative algorithm focused on unity, rhythm, and movement. Sixteen unique iterations were produced from Return Zero [Blue].

Artist: Tyler Hobbs  
Year: 2021  
Medium: Algorithmic Design  
Dimensions: 10800 x 10800 px

This work was exhibited as part of "Transformations" at INSTITUT in 2022.

Ownership of this NFT entitles the holder to purchase a signed, high quality print and source code print-out from Tyler Hobbs for a reasonable fee.

tyler-hobbs-2-9345.name: Elektroanima 0.9
tyler-hobbs-2-9345.description:
Elektroanima is an investigation into combining hand-drawing and algorithmic processes. Each work in this series began as a curve drawn by hand. These curves were transformed by custom algorithms into the final works. Sixteen unique iterations were produced from Elektroanima.

Artist: Tyler Hobbs  
Year: 2020/2022  
Medium: Hybrid hand-drawn and algorithmic design  
Dimensions: 10800 x 10800 px

This work was exhibited as part of "Synaesthesia" at INSTITUT in 2022.

Ownership of this NFT entitles the holder to purchase a signed, high quality print and source code print-out from Tyler Hobbs for a reasonable fee.

tyler-hobbs-3-9345.name: One One Overflow
tyler-hobbs-3-9345.description:
The inspiration for One One Overflow came from the meeting point of digital precision and analogue unpredictability. The core building block of the algorithm that generated this image is quite simple: try to place sequential dots along a line. What makes the results interesting are its departures from the perfect form. Small errors stack up and accumulate, cascading into divergent and unpredictable artifacts. This type of variation happens naturally in a medium like painting, but when working through an algorithm, it becomes a rare blessing.

Artist: Tyler Hobbs  
Year: 2022  
Medium: Algorithmic Design  
Dimensions: 10000 x 12500 px

This work was exhibited as part of "In Our Code" at Unit London in 2022.

Ownership of this NFT entitles the holder to purchase a signed, high quality print and source code print-out from Tyler Hobbs for a reasonable fee.

tyler-hobbs-2-8e82.name: Harbor Scene #2 (after John Henry Twachtman)
tyler-hobbs-2-8e82.description:
"Harbor Scene (after John Henry Twachtman)" is a series of three unique iterations produced from a single generative algorithm.

This work is part of a group project, Remembrance of Things Future, in collaboration with LACMA and Cactoid Labs.

The initiative invited artists to respond to works in the museum's permanent collection, bridging the past with the present through digital art and blockchain technology.

Artist: Tyler Hobbs  
Year: 2024  
Medium: Algorithmic Design  
Dimensions: 15000 x 18000 px

winds-of-yawanawa-20-100b.name: Winds of Yawanawa #20 by Yawanawa and Refik Anadol
winds-of-yawanawa-20-100b.description:
Winds of Yawanawa is a collection of 1000 unique Data Paintings that harness weather data from the tribe’s village in the Amazon rainforest including wind speed, gusts, direction and temperature. This data then merges with the works of young Yawanawa artists, resulting in a mesmerizing play of traditional shapes and colors of data pigmentation.

The Winds of Yawanawa collection will bring the nuances of Yawanawa art to the digital world with the aim of preserving their rich culture.

NFT Data Paintings
Format: MP4 | 60 second loop

Each NFT comes with an archival museum quality physical print (44x22 inches) signed by Chief Nixiwaka Yawanawa & Refik Anadol
